channel.title: Opensource Sunday – Matthew 7
channel.description: Recent content in Matthew 7 on Opensource Sunday

item.title: Lesson 7 · Judge Not
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 7:1–6 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

Matthew 7:1 is the most often-quoted verse by people who don’t actually go to church. “Do not judge, or you too will be judged.” It gets used to mean “don’t ever say anything is right or wrong” — which is almost the opposite of what Jesus is saying.

Here’s what Jesus actually packs into these six verses:

  • Verse 1–2: “Do not judge, or you too will be judged. For in the same way you judge others, you will be judged, and with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.” Jesus isn’t banning moral discernment — he literally just told us (earlier in the sermon) to watch for false prophets and guard our hearts. What he’s banning is the posture of the judge. The smug, stand-above, contempt kind of judging. He’s warning: whatever yardstick you use on people will come back around on you.
  • Verses 3–5 — The Speck and the Plank. This is the comedic-on-purpose centerpiece. Jesus paints a ridiculous picture: you have a two-by-four sticking out of your face and you’re leaning in to fix a speck of sawdust in someone else’s eye. It’s absurd. It’s meant to be. Jesus is not saying “never help your brother or sister” — he says “first take the plank out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.” Self-examination before correction, not instead of it.
  • Verse 6 — “Do not give dogs what is sacred; do not throw your pearls to pigs.” This feels like a sudden pivot, but Jesus is balancing his point. He’s not saying “accept everything, call nothing wrong.” He’s saying: use discernment about what’s precious. Some people aren’t ready to hear truth, and forcing it can do harm. Judgment is out; discernment is in.

The core idea for middle schoolers: you can care enough about someone to help them see clearly — but first you have to deal with what you’re not seeing about yourself. The move isn’t don’t judge anything. The move is don’t judge from a throne you don’t belong on.

Middle school is a ruthless ranking machine. Judgment is the native language. Jesus is inviting a different posture — humble, self-aware, merciful.

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

The way you judge is the way you’ll be judged. Check the plank in your own eye before you go after the speck in anyone else’s.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • A 2×4 piece of wood (or even a yardstick — something obviously big) and a tiny speck of something (a single grain of sawdust, a crumb, a pencil shaving)
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight’s lesson cuts — ask the Holy Spirit to make us less interested in other people’s specks and more honest about our own planks.

Catch Their Attention — Plank in Your Eye

Before the lesson: Have the 2×4 (or yardstick) lying in plain sight. Place a tiny crumb or grain of something on a napkin nearby.

During the lesson: Hold up the tiny speck dramatically.

Okay. Everyone look really hard at this. What is it?

Let them try to identify it. Then pick up the 2×4.

Now. Imagine I have this sticking out of my face. Like, right in my eye. Right now. Big 2×4, just… bam, in the eye. And instead of dealing with that, I’m over here trying to pick that tiny speck out of your eyelashes. How do you think that’s gonna go?

Let them laugh. Make it goofy.

Hold onto that picture. Jesus actually tells this joke tonight. And he means it.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 7:1–6

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

Matthew 7:1 is the most misused verse of the entire Bible. You’ve probably heard someone quote it to mean “don’t ever say anything is right or wrong.” That’s not what Jesus is saying. Listen carefully — Jesus is going after a specific kind of judging, and he’s hilarious about it.

Read Matthew 7:1–6 together.

Discuss:

Verses 1–2:

  1. What does Jesus warn is going to happen to us if we judge others harshly? — The same measure gets used on us. The yardstick you hold up on others will come back and land on you.
  2. What does that tell us about what kind of judging Jesus is against? — The smug, above-it-all kind. Not the kind that says “that’s not right.” The kind that says “…and I would never.”

Verses 3–5: 3. Draw the picture Jesus is painting. — A person with a 2×4 sticking out of their face, leaning in to help someone with a tiny piece of sawdust in their eye. 4. Why do you think Jesus makes this sound so ridiculous? — Because it IS. We do it all the time, and it’s absurd. We’ll rip on someone for a flaw we’re twice as guilty of. 5. Jesus doesn’t say “never help your brother.” What does he say? — First take the plank out of your own eye. Then you’ll see clearly to help.

Verse 6: 6. This one sounds weird after the speck-and-plank stuff. What does it balance? — Jesus isn’t saying “never discern anything.” He’s saying: use wisdom about what’s precious. Don’t force truth on people who aren’t ready. Judgment is out; discernment is in.

Say:

Here’s what Jesus is doing. He is not saying, “never call anything wrong.” He’s saying, “drop the gavel.” The seat of judgment is not for you. It’s not for me. It’s for God.

That doesn’t mean we don’t care about what’s right. It doesn’t mean we let our friends crash. It just means — start with yourself. When you feel the urge to go after someone else, first ask: what’s the plank I’m carrying? What am I doing that’s way bigger than what I’m upset about?

Because here’s the wild thing Jesus promises. Once you pull your plank? You’ll actually see clearly enough to help. Judgment makes you blind. Humility makes you a good friend.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. Have you ever had somebody call you out for something when they were clearly doing the same thing ten times worse? How did that feel?
  2. What’s a “plank” you’ve been carrying that’s way bigger than the “specks” you’re always noticing in other people?
  3. Middle school is built on judgment — roasts, rankings, the unspoken lunch-table pecking order. What would it look like if you refused to play?
  4. Is there someone you’ve been quietly judging this week whose story you don’t actually know?
  5. Jesus says “the measure you use will be measured to you.” If someone used YOUR yardstick on YOU this week, how would it go?

Turn Toward Action — Plank Check

Hand each student an index card. Say:

Two sides of the card. One side is honest. One side is the move.

Side 1:

  1. One speck I’ve been harping on in someone else’s life.
  2. One plank I’ve been ignoring in my own.

Side 2: 3. One thing I’m going to do this week to pull my plank — apologize, confess, get help, talk to someone, pray about it honestly.

If you’ve been harping on someone else, the card is also a little reminder: pull your plank first.

Close with prayer. Ask God for eyes that see our own stuff clearly and eyes that are slower to judge everyone else.

item.title: Lesson 8 · Ask, Seek, Knock
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 7:7–12 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

Six verses. Three huge ideas. Jesus is bringing the Sermon on the Mount in for a landing, and the thread holding these verses together is one word: Father.

  • Verses 7–8 — Ask. Seek. Knock. “Ask and it will be given to you; seek and you will find; knock and the door will be opened to you.” All three verbs in the Greek are present tense and imperative — keep asking, keep seeking, keep knocking. Jesus isn’t describing a one-time prayer formula. He’s describing a posture of ongoing dependence.
  • Verses 9–11 — The Father Logic. “Which of you, if your son asks for bread, will give him a stone? Or if he asks for a fish, will give him a snake? If you, then, though you are evil, know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good gifts to those who ask him!” This is the heart of the passage. Jesus argues from the lesser to the greater. Even broken, imperfect human parents instinctively give their kids bread, not stones. How much more will your perfect heavenly Father give what you need? He’s not saying we’ll always get what we want — he’s saying we’ll get good gifts, because our Father knows what’s good.
  • Verse 12 — The Golden Rule. “So in everything, do to others what you would have them do to you, for this sums up the Law and the Prophets.” The “so” is crucial. Because we have a Father who gives good gifts — because we’re kids who have received generosity we didn’t earn — we can afford to treat others with the same generosity. The Golden Rule isn’t a standalone moral principle. It’s the overflow of being a child of a generous Father.

Two pastoral things to hold:

  1. Jesus is not teaching a vending-machine gospel. “Ask and it will be given” doesn’t mean every prayer gets a yes. It means God hears, and God answers with good gifts — gifts shaped by his wisdom, not our wish list.
  2. The “Father” language here is the same loaded word you flagged in Lesson 4. Don’t skip past it. This entire passage rests on the premise that God is a good Father — and some of your students need to hear, again, that this is the Father they actually have.

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

You have a good Father who loves to be asked. And because you’ve received so much, you can afford to treat other people the way you want to be treated.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • A loaf of bread + a rock + a gummy fish + a rubber snake (for the Father-logic visual). Bread + stone is the minimum; fish + snake doubles the impact.
  • A closed door to knock on (literally) for the hook
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. Tonight’s passage is about a Father who loves being asked — so actually ask him to speak to this group tonight.

Catch Their Attention — Knock Knock

Before the lesson: Pick a door the group can see. If you have a co-leader, have them wait on the other side.

During the lesson: Point at the door. Say:

I want to try something. [Pick a student.] Stand up. Walk over to that door. Now — knock on it like you really want to get in.

Let them knock. The leader on the other side opens the door and says warmly: “Come on in.”

That was easy. Now imagine you spent years never knocking on that door. Never asking to be let in. Never even walking up. How weird would that be — if the person on the other side was ready to open it the whole time?

Say:

Jesus is about to tell us something about a door. Your Father is on the other side of it. He’s been waiting. You can knock.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 7:7–12

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

Six verses. Jesus is wrapping up the Sermon on the Mount, and he packs a TON into these six. Listen for one word he keeps coming back to: Father. Everything in this passage hinges on it.

Read Matthew 7:7–12 together.

Discuss:

Verses 7–8 — Ask, Seek, Knock:

  1. What three things does Jesus tell us to do? — Ask. Seek. Knock.
  2. Fun fact: in the original Greek, all three of these are verbs that mean keep doing it. Keep asking. Keep seeking. Keep knocking. Why do you think Jesus frames prayer as ongoing? — Because prayer isn’t a one-time form you submit. It’s a relationship. You don’t text your best friend once and call it a friendship.

Verses 9–11 — The Father Logic: 3. Jesus uses a ridiculous example. Read verse 9 again. What does he ask? — If your kid asks for bread, are you gonna hand them a rock?

Hold up the bread and the rock. Make it stupid and obvious.

  1. Why does Jesus use such an absurd example? — Because no decent parent in the world would do that. The question is supposed to be embarrassingly easy.
  2. Then Jesus says “IF YOU, THOUGH YOU ARE EVIL, know how to give good gifts…” What does that mean? — Even broken humans instinctively give their kids good stuff. How much more will a perfect Father do?
  3. What’s the “how much more” Jesus is arguing for? — If broken parents still give good gifts, God — the perfect Father — will absolutely give good gifts to those who ask.

Pastoral note — the “Father” word again. Like in Lesson 4, don’t rush past this. If some students hear “Father” and flinch, name it out loud:

Some of us have dads who are amazing. Some of us have dads who aren’t. Jesus is not saying God is like your dad. He’s saying God is the Father every one of you was made to have. The kind who gives bread, not stones. Every time.

  1. Does “ask and it will be given” mean I always get what I want? — No. It means God gives good gifts. Sometimes what you asked for wasn’t actually good. God’s answers are shaped by his wisdom, not your wish list. But he always hears.

Verse 12 — The Golden Rule: 8. Now Jesus says “SO” — which means because of what he just said. How does the Golden Rule flow out of the Father stuff? — Because you’ve received generosity from your Father, you can afford to be generous with other people. Because you’ve been forgiven, you can forgive. You treat others the way you want to be treated because you’ve been treated that way first. 9. Jesus says “this sums up the Law and the Prophets.” That’s a huge claim. What does he mean? — He’s saying: the whole Old Testament boils down to this. Love God. Treat people the way you want to be treated. That’s it.

Say:

Jesus is handing us one of the most famous lines ever said — the Golden Rule. Do to others what you would have them do to you. You’ve heard it on posters, bumper stickers, maybe Ms. Whoever’s kindergarten wall. But notice where Jesus puts it. Right after he tells us our Father gives good gifts.

Because that’s where the Golden Rule actually works. When you’ve been given more than you deserve, you stop trying to ration what you give. When you know you have a generous Father, you can afford to be generous. When you’ve been loved first, you can love back.

Ask. Seek. Knock. And then overflow into the people around you.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. What’s something you’ve stopped asking God for because you don’t really think he’d answer? Why?
  2. When you pray, do you treat God more like a vending machine, a distant boss, or a good Father? Why?
  3. Who in your life treats you better than you deserve? What does it feel like?
  4. The Golden Rule in a middle-school hallway — what would change if you actually did that this week?
  5. Jesus says the Golden Rule sums up the whole Old Testament. If it sums up yours — how are you doing?

Turn Toward Action — Ask and Overflow

Hand each student an index card. Say:

Two sides tonight.

Side 1 — What I’m asking for: Write down one real thing you’re going to start asking, seeking, and knocking about this week. Something specific. Then commit: every morning or every night, bring this to your Father.

Side 2 — How I’m overflowing: Write down one specific person this week — and one thing you’re going to do for them that you’d want someone to do for you. Sit with them. Stand up for them. Text them. Forgive them. Include them.

Close with prayer. Before the closing prayer, let a few students say — if they want to — what they’re asking God for. Pray over those out loud.

End with:

Father, you love being asked. Your gifts are always good. Teach this group to keep knocking. And because we’ve received so much from you, help us overflow into every hallway this week. Amen.

item.title: Lesson 9 · House on the Rock
item.description:
Scripture: Matthew 7:24–29 Series: Sermon on the Mount

Behind the Curtain

What do I need to know before teaching?

This is the capstone. For eight weeks we’ve been climbing the mountain with Jesus — the Beatitudes, salt and light, the heart of the Law, prayer, treasure, trust, judgment, and asking. Tonight Jesus steps up to close his own sermon, and the way he wraps it is devastatingly simple: a parable about two builders.

Matthew 7:24–27 — both builders hear the same words. One puts them into practice and builds on the rock. The other hears but doesn’t act and builds on the sand. The storm comes for both. One house stands. The other falls with a crash.

A few things to hold onto before you teach:

  • The line between the wise and foolish builder is NOT knowledge. Both builders heard Jesus’ words. The line is practice. Hearing plus doing = rock. Hearing minus doing = sand.
  • The storm is a given. Jesus doesn’t say if the rain falls — he says when. Every life gets storms. Middle school has plenty already; adulthood brings more. The question is never whether the rain comes. It’s always what your house is built on.
  • Verse 28–29 is the crowd’s reaction. “When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were amazed at his teaching, because he taught as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law.” Jesus didn’t just quote other rabbis. He spoke with the authority of the one these words came from in the first place.

Series recap to weave in:

Lesson Passage The thread
1 · Up the Mountain Matt 5:1–12 Who God calls blessed
2 · Salt and Light Matt 5:13–16 Be flavor and illumination
3 · But I Say Matt 5:17–48 Heart, not just behavior
4 · When You Pray Matt 6:5–15 Talk to your Father
5 · Treasures and Two Masters Matt 6:19–24 What has your heart has your life
6 · Do Not Worry Matt 6:25–34 Trust the One who feeds sparrows
7 · Judge Not Matt 7:1–6 Check the plank before the speck
8 · Ask, Seek, Knock Matt 7:7–12 A good Father hears his kids
9 · House on the Rock Matt 7:24–29 Hear AND do. Build on Jesus.

Jesus has been showing students a new way to be human. Tonight, he asks: are you going to build your life on it?

Center Stage

What are the most important takeaways?

Jesus isn’t asking us to follow the rules harder — he’s inviting us to build our whole life on him. Hear AND do.

Prop Department

What supplies do I need?

  • Bibles, one per student
  • A Jenga tower (or Lego bricks, or a small stack of books) — for the building demo
  • Whiteboard or large paper + markers
  • Index cards — one per student
  • Pens/pencils
  • Christ light / candle + matches (for opening)
  • Optional: a speaker + “New Way to Be Human” (Switchfoot) cued up for the close

Directing the Scene

What learning activities might we do?

Start with Prayer

Light the Christ light. Invite a student to lead in prayer. This is the last lesson of a nine-week series — ask God to help us not just finish the Sermon on the Mount, but build our lives on it.

Catch Their Attention — The Building Test

Before the lesson: Have a Jenga tower built and ready on the table. (If you don’t have Jenga, stack books, Legos, or Solo cups.)

During the lesson: Gesture at the tower. Say:

This tower is solid. Nobody’s touching it. But — what would happen if I started pulling pieces from the bottom?

Pull a block from the bottom row. Then another. Keep going until it wobbles. Let a student knock it down if they want.

Then rebuild a tower and ask:

What if instead we pulled pieces from the top? Very different story, right?

What everything is built on matters more than what’s on top. Tonight — on our last night in the Sermon on the Mount — Jesus is going to tell us a story about exactly that.

Read and Interpret — Matthew 7:24–29

Intro for the youth: Before reading, say:

We’ve been climbing this mountain for eight weeks. We heard who God calls blessed. We got called salt and light. We got pushed past the rules to the heart. We learned how to pray. We got warned about trying to serve two masters. We learned how to trust instead of worry. We got told to check our own plank before we go after anyone’s speck. We heard that our Father loves to be asked. Tonight Jesus wraps it all up. Listen for the moment the whole series comes to a point.

Read Matthew 7:24–29 together. Go around the circle. Read it slowly.

Discuss:

  1. What’s the ONE difference between the wise builder and the foolish builder? — Both hear Jesus’ words. Only one puts them into practice.
  2. Do both houses get the storm? — Yes. Jesus doesn’t say if. He says when.
  3. What are the rains, streams, and winds in real life? — Middle school drama. Family stuff. Losses. Anxiety. The hard year. The heartbreak. The thing that blindsides you.
  4. Why does the wise builder’s house stand? — Because of what it’s built on. The foundation.
  5. The foolish builder isn’t evil. He’s not a pagan. He heard Jesus’ same words. What’s his problem? — He never did anything about them. He filed them away. Information without action.
  6. Look at verses 28–29. What’s the crowd’s reaction when Jesus finishes the whole sermon? — They’re amazed. Because Jesus doesn’t quote other teachers. He speaks like he’s the one the words come from.

Say:

Here’s the punchline Jesus chose to close with. Not a bow. Not a blessing. A parable with teeth.

Everybody builds on something. Grades. Sports. Looks. Friends. Followers. Money. Achievement. Parents’ approval. Being funny. All of those things can be good — but not one of them can hold a life together when the storm rolls in.

And Jesus isn’t saying “listen to me, memorize me, share me, post about me.” He’s saying hear my words AND do them. Hearing without doing is sand. Hearing plus doing is rock.

That’s the whole series in one sentence: Jesus isn’t asking us to try harder at religion. He’s inviting us to build our whole life on him.

Series Recap — Eight Weeks in Ninety Seconds

Before Ponder, flash back through the series fast on the whiteboard. Write each one up as you say it:

  • Lesson 1 — Up the Mountain. Jesus calls who blessed? Not who the world calls blessed. The poor in spirit, the mourners, the meek, the merciful, the peacemakers, the persecuted.
  • Lesson 2 — Salt and Light. You ARE salt. You ARE light. Your life is supposed to flavor and illuminate the world.
  • Lesson 3 — But I Say. Jesus doesn’t just want your behavior. He wants your heart.
  • Lesson 4 — When You Pray. You have a Father. Talk to him like a kid. Short. Honest. Trusting.
  • Lesson 5 — Treasures and Two Masters. Whatever has your heart has your life. You can’t serve God and money — pick one.
  • Lesson 6 — Do Not Worry. Your Father feeds sparrows. He’s got you. Seek first his kingdom.
  • Lesson 7 — Judge Not. Check your own plank before you go after anyone’s speck.
  • Lesson 8 — Ask, Seek, Knock. Your Father loves to be asked. Treat people the way you want to be treated.

Point back at the Jenga tower.

Every one of those isn’t a brick to balance on top. They’re the foundation. That’s what Jesus is telling us to build on.

Ponder the Possibilities

Discuss together:

  1. Of the eight earlier lessons, which one has been hardest for you to actually put into practice?
  2. What are some things middle schoolers try to build their lives on — sand that looks solid until the storm comes?
  3. Has a storm ever hit you and you realized you were building on sand? What happened?
  4. Jesus is super clear: hearing without doing is not enough. Is there something from these nine weeks you’ve heard but not actually done?
  5. What would it look like to build your middle-school life on Jesus? Not someday. This week.

Turn Toward Action — What Are You Building On?

Hand each student an index card. Say:

Last Sermon on the Mount homework of the series. Three questions. Be honest.

  1. What am I building my life on right now? (The honest answer. Not the Sunday-school answer.)
  2. What do I want to be building on?
  3. ONE thing I’ll do this week to lay a brick on Jesus. (Pray the Lord’s Prayer every morning. Reconcile with that person. Refuse to join that gossip chain. Hand that worry to God. Say hi to that kid nobody sits with.)

Keep the card. Put it in your Bible, on your mirror, in your locker, in your phone case.

Close together. If you have the speaker cued up, play “New Way to Be Human” by Switchfoot as students finish writing. Pray together:

Jesus, we don’t want to build on sand. We’ve heard you for nine weeks. Help us do you for the next sixty years. Thank you for showing us a new way to be human. Make us wise builders. Make our lives stand on you. Amen.

Stand at the door as students leave. Say each student’s name and bless them out:

"[Name], you’ve been loved, you’ve belonged, and you’ve been made to make a difference. Now go build on the rock."
